Name: rate_distortion_based_model_compression
Title: Rate-Distortion Optimized Model Compression: Beyond Heuristics
Short Hypothesis: This research proposes a novel approach to model compression that directly connects the quantization and pruning processes to rate-distortion theory, allowing for precise calculations of the achievable bit-cost while preserving predictive accuracy. This approach contrasts with current methods that often rely on heuristic or empirical tuning, which lack a clear theoretical foundation and do not guarantee optimality in terms of information preservation.
Related Work: Existing literature primarily focuses on heuristic methods for model compression that lack strong theoretical underpinnings (e.g., mixed-precision techniques). While there is some work on rate-distortion in compression (e.g., 'Multiple-Description Coding' and 'Lossy Compression with Gaussian Diffusion'), these approaches do not apply the rate-distortion framework explicitly to model compression strategies in a way that quantifies bit-cost relative to task performance. This proposal is novel in its explicit framing of compression as a rate-distortion problem that yields testable predictions.
Abstract: In this proposal, we present a new approach to model compression that leverages the principles of rate-distortion theory to optimize quantization and pruning strategies. We frame model compression as a lossy source coding problem, where the goal is to minimize the bit-cost for the model while maintaining predictive accuracy within an acceptable distortion threshold. By applying information-theoretic bounds, we derive achievable rates and establish a framework for evaluating the compressibility of neural networks based on their mutual information with the task. Our approach contrasts with existing heuristic-based methods by providing a rigorous mathematical foundation, allowing us to formulate explicit predictions regarding the trade-offs between model size and performance. We will test our theory through a series of experiments, validating our bounds and demonstrating the practical applicability of our compression framework across various neural architectures.
Experiments: 1. Apply the proposed rate-distortion framework to quantize weights of a standard neural network (e.g., ResNet or LSTM) while measuring distortion in terms of task-specific accuracy. 2. Implement pruning strategies where the pruning rate is determined by the derived rate-distortion bounds. Measure the effect on predictive performance and validate against theoretical predictions. 3. Evaluate different levels of quantization and pruning on benchmark datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and quantify the resulting distortion-rate curves. Metrics: accuracy, bit-cost (bits-per-weight), and visualizations of distortion-rate trade-offs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: This approach may encounter difficulties in accurately estimating mutual information and distortion in practical scenarios, particularly in high-dimensional spaces where overfitting to training data can occur. The reliance on theoretical bounds may also limit applicability to specific model architectures and datasets, necessitating further empirical validation across diverse conditions.
